speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I am in Auschwitz, trying to learn the ropes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I mentioned it, there was a hierarchy here. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- starting from the top down to the block leaders and the, the room leaders-- and their functions were mainly their own self-preservation, you know. They didn't worry about anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if a block elder knew that he has, say, I'm not sure-- I can't remember how many people were in a block now, but say, 2,000. So he would go to the kitchen every day and draw rations for 2,000 people. And the rations that were really the most worthwhile were the evening meal, when you-- when they drew bread and margarine for us. Because the rest was liquid stuff only. So they decided, when they received this bread and it was brought down to their room, they decided how thin to slice this bread, you see. And this was the scam, you know. Instead of 10, 10 slices out of a bread, they cut 20 slices. That meant that they had six, or 10, or how many loaves of bread left. And they were trading this around, you know, for cigarettes or what have you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just wanted to tell you that the Polish people who were there, besides that, they were allowed to get a parcel, one parcel a month, you know. Because their families were back at home, you know, where us Jews, you know, we were all-- our families were gone, you know. We had nobody to send us parcels, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was the hierarchy. And, as I said, they had good clothes, they had handmade, tailored outfits, you know, even though they were stripes. But it was tailored for them, because there were tailor shops there that were getting all these clothes, the clothes that were taken away from the prisoners were cleaned here and fixed and sent to Germany. So this was the camp hierarchy. And as far as myself, the ropes that I had to learn, you had to know right away that you-- what life was all about here. It was, it was hard. And you had to really watch out for yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I learned fast that you should never stick out in the crowd, you know. You had to avoid a bad beating. Because if you received a bad beating, that was the end of you, you know. I mean, they could kill you on the spot, and you had to avoid this at any cost, you know. So you sort of learned how not to stick in the eye of any-- of either the kapo or an SS guard. This was your most important thing. And I wound up in the top bunk. Because the people that had-- they were the stronger people. And you know, they were able to get the lower bunks. And I had to climb to the top, which at the beginning, it wasn't really that, you know, difficult for me. Later on, climbing to the top bunk was becoming a little more difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are, lined up. The next day comes around. And we are marched out to our work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we-- first of all, in the morning, we had to get up very early in the morning. We were given a-- this cup of tea again. And after tea, we had to get downstairs and line up to, to be counted. It was called appell. Every morning we were counted, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine-- I don't know how many people were in Auschwitz, maybe 40,000-- and every barrack was lined up near the barrack. And the block leader had to get the full count. And then the [GERMAN], the Lager writer-- who was also a prisoner, a political prisoner-- he got the total. And it was all totaled up, and it was handed over to the SS Lager-- the camp-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the head of the SS, who was the head of the camp. So you can imagine if somebody dropped dead somewhere, and he was somewhere-- I don't know, they had-- we were standing on appell until this body was found. And then they had to drag this body out, and stand them up, so that there was a count, you see. And many times, we were standing for hours, until they found a dead body somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was an extra hardship after a full day's work, you know, to stand. When we came back, as I said, we were counted in the morning, when we went out. And when we came back from the work details, we were counted again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are, marching out to work. And as we go through the gates, on each side there are SS guards, picking us up on each side, as we go out. And we go to our workplace. And we would march some days five, six, seven kilometers to our workplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what we were doing was we were at the-- initially, we were leveling fields, grounds, you know, to make it level, so they can grow some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wheat or I think they were growing a lot of mustard in that area. I remember a lot of these yellow flowers on the mustard. They were making oil out of this. And I, I think part of this mustard, the stems, were put into our soup. Because they only use the top part, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very hard work. We had to work with pick axes and shovels, you know, and level fields, and grounds. And we worked from morning till noon. It was very hot, and there was no drinking water. It was a very swampy area, and we used to drink the groundwater. And we got very sick, you know. We had dysentery and diarrhea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And lunchtime came. And we were-- they brought the soup. And-- this soup was mostly water, really. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was really not enough food to, to eat, you know, after doing this kind of work. And I think we got a half an hour lunch. And I remember all the SS were sitting around in a perimeter. And I remember watching the SSs, they took out from their packs, you know, their bread and their bacon, you know. And they were eating that. And you know, our stomachs we're just groaning from hunger, you know. And they were giving scraps to their dogs. I mean, the dogs had better food than we had, you know, for sure. This was very hard to take. And then it was back to work. And we worked till about 6:30 to 7 o'clock, depending, you know, how far away we were from camp. Nonstop, you know, again, from lunchtime till about 6 o'clock. And then it was a march back to camp. And they-- the kapos, you know, they drove us-- we had to march in a military fashion. And eventually, you know, we had to sing German songs, you know. It had to just go like so. And it came-- we were back to camp. And as we came back to camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to clean and polish our shoes and line up for appell. They had some, some black stuff, it looked like some grease. We had to smear it on our boots, so it looked nice and clean, which was fine, you know. This was great. In Auschwitz, you could do that. Because we had-- where the latrines were, in the barrack, in this big common room, in the middle there was a-- that's where the water taps were, with a trough, you know. I mean, there were hundreds of-- I don't know, a couple hundred taps there, so that you could wash from either side, you know. And that was great. And I remember there were signs in Auschwitz, you know, a big sign that one louse-- you know, lice, you know-- could be your death, you know. So you know, you tried hard, you know, you-- but we had to wash our own jackets and stuff like that, you know. We didn't get any clean underwear. There was no underwear, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we came back at night, we received our rations. And supper was a cup of coffee. We call it ersatz coffee, you know. It looked like coffee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we received a slice of bread that was about a quarter inch thick-- that was mostly, it was crumbling away-- and a little square of margarine. And this was our diet. Some nights instead of margarine, they gave us a little spoon of something that looked like jam, you know. It was made from some artificial stuff. And this was our diet, day in and day out. On Sunday, lunch was the main meal. At lunch time, we had a soup, and they gave about three, four little potatoes like that. And this was the diet. And from what I gather, it was less than 300 calories a day. So you know, we were so hungry. After a week or two, you know, the hunger was so bad that-- and, you know, I had a dilemma, what to do with this bread every night, you know. Because if I ate the whole piece, you know, I had nothing left for the next day. So I had to decide that I'm only taking a couple of little bites. And I had a [NON-ENGLISH], a rag, you know, that I used to roll this bread into this rag and keep it for the next day. And you know, I had to guard against people stealing this. So the most important things that your life depended on were your boots and was this [NON-ENGLISH], this metal dish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every night when I went to sleep, I took my boots off. And I put this bread with the rag inside the boots. And I tied the shoelace through the hole in this metal dish. And I tied the shoelaces to my wrist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put everything under my head. And that that's the way I slept every night. In case somebody wants to steal it, you know, I'll feel the tug, you know. Because you know, people would, would kill you for a crumb, you know. I mean, there was-- absolutely humanity was gone. And I was very lucky that I was with my father and my uncle, you know. And you know, we tried, you know. I've seen sons and fathers or fathers and sons, you know, fight each other for a piece of bread. And I know my father and my uncle, they would give me half their rations every day, just to keep me going, you know. And so, we worked together for a few months on, on this work detail, on this [NON-ENGLISH] commando.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type--what different types of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was draining swamps. And then, I was-- we had to put fertilizer on these-- where the swamps were drained. And I, I was on a detail for about three, four weeks. This was a bad one. We had to put this lime-- it was a chemical that we had to throw on the drained swamps, to fertilize it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember, we came out on this detail. And there was this mountain of white powder, you know, sitting there. And there were hundreds of baskets, you know, like baskets that high. Like a bushel basket, sort of.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were guys with shovels lined up and with the rest of us with baskets. And they filled up our baskets with this chemical. And where it was rain or shine, you know, We have to carry-- oh gee, this rhymes, rain or shine, we had to carry this lime, you know-- this chemical. And it was heavy. So from this pile where we picked it up, we put it on our shoulder, and we went to the field. And, you know, 150 of us, we lined up in sort of like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a staggered way. And then we took the basket off our shoulder and put it on our knees. And we scooped it up with our hand and just sprayed it on the field, you see. And when the basket was gone, you went back for another load. At this went on, you know, day on and day on. And after about three, four days, this chemical, this lime, ate-- started to eat away the skin on our fingers. And I remember, I could see the bone sticking out on my fingers. And my kneecap was visible, because the skin was all-- it was actually rotting away. And this stuff was leaking. Because if it was raining, you know, this stuff was leaking and leaching out from the baskets. And our outfits, our skins, we were all covered with this white lime. And our skins were itching, you know. We couldn't wash this off. And this was a terrible time, you know, to-- I'll never forget that we look like white ghosts, you know. You could never wash this off. And so when we were finished working, in the evening, the SS guards, to have fun with us, they allowed us to run into these ponds that were not far from there, to wash ourselves off. So what they did is they unleashed these big guard dogs. And they sicced them on us. And we ran like crazy, into the water, you know. And if a bunch of people jumped on you, you know, you could, you could drown, you know, under the weight of these people. So-- but I was able to-- I was able to swim. And I remember one day I swam into the reeds. And I found two duck eggs. Because there were some ponds there. They were raising ducks somewhere, not far from there. And I found these two duck eggs in this reed. And that, I thought, was a great find. And I remember, I had one egg right away. And I thought, I'll bring the other one back to camp and give it to my father and uncle. I was just-- I want to backtrack. We worked together for quite a while, with my father and uncle. But they realized that as soon as the kapos or the SS commandant, who was the commandant of our group. I remember his name. His name was scharfuhrer, which is sergeant, Scharfuhrer Kunz. He was an Austrian. This man was about six foot four, a very skinny type of a guy. He was-- he had his pistol was always hanging down from his belt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So as soon as they noticed that there was a family connection, a father and a son, they would start to beat one or the other. Because they did not want to have anything like this. They wanted to destroy you, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was actually-- there was no humanity left, you know. They did not want you to have even that little bit of support, you see. So my father said that we're going to separate. Because it's very difficult to see, if they beat me or they beat him, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did separate. And they went to work in another detail. They went to work in a factory that was there. It was called Union Ammunition Works.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, many of the prisoners here worked in the big factories all around Auschwitz, like RG Farben and Siemens and many others. So I was separated from them. And when I used to come back at night, our group came back the last. Because we have to march from long distances away. And my father and uncle used to wait for me inside the gates. And we used to have supper together. And they always made sure that I was OK. And they gave me something, you know. If they managed to find a crumb somewhere or something, they, they used to give it to me. Many times, when I walked-- when we walked back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was the first time that I was really shocked, the first few weeks when I was in Auschwitz. You know, sometimes people tried to escape from there. It was very difficult to escape. You know, if you could escape from your work detail and you hid somewhere, you had to fight your way through three cordons, you know, of guard towers. So it was not simply running away from your work detail. You had to get through all these three cordons. Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when these fellows were caught, they were hanged right away, in Auschwitz. So I remember the first time when I came in from the work detail, and there was this fellow hanging inside the gate, and that was a shock to see it. And with a big plaque here-- this is what happens to people that run away, you know. And after that, I've seen many times people that were trying to steal some food from the kitchen, and they were caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: At appell, you know, when we were being counted, you know, at, at night, when all these 40,000 people were standing, this guy was brought in front there. And he was tied up on a drum, sort of, like he was bent over. His hands were tied down and his feet. And this fellow was a huge guy. And he came, and he gave him 25 lashes. And once you got 25 lashes, that was your end, you know. You'd never survive that anymore. And we had to all watch this type of things and you know, other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was separate from my-- you know, I was on a separate detail. Other details I was working on, we had to carry these cement and steel pylons that were used around for electrified fences. Three of us had to carry this, you know. It was a-- you know, this square cement pole, you know. And it was sort of bent at the end, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how heavy this was, but three of us had to carry this. And you know, it was sitting on my shoulder, on the bones. And I thought that this will surely kill me, you know. And how I managed to do this for a week or two, I really don't know. We took one, and then we went back for another one, you know. And it just kept going, you know. And that was another detail that I was working on. This is summer is gone, and the fall is coming. And October of 1944. You know, when the Allies landed in Normandy, we got sort of a bit of a, a hint of this. I remember the guards were talking to each other. And, you know, we were never able to see a newspaper. But when the guards were reading the newspapers, and sometimes they either discarded a paper, and somebody picked it up, and they could read what was happening, so. But you know, to me, Normandy was so far away, I couldn't even worry about it, you know. Because I was right here. And you know, that was really of no concern, you know. My lot did not get any better or whatever, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember in October, I was working on a farm. This time we were working with potatoes. It was, I think, middle of October. And what we were doing, we were stacking potatoes in a big pit, storing it away for the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big farm. And we were taking these potatoes on wheelbarrows. And I was able to snatch a potato every now and then and just eat it raw, like that. If they caught you eating a potato, you could have been shot on the spot, because it was called sabotage. So you had to be very careful what you were doing. We carted this with wheelbarrows into this pit. And once the pit was full, it was covered with a lot of straw. And then dirt was put on top, so it will not freeze during the winter. And this was in an afternoon. And we heard a siren from Birkenau. This was outside Birkenau, about two kilometers from Birkenau. And we heard a siren from Birkenau. And usually the sound of a siren meant that there was an escape. One siren, one blow of the siren-- and that meant to warn the guards on the towers all around, you know, the three-- it was called a [GERMAN], the post chain. Post [GERMAN] is a chain, and post is the tower. There were three [GERMAN] to warn them to be on their toes, because there is an escape. And all of a sudden, we could hear shooting and yelling and dogs barking, you know. And this sound was coming closer to where we were working. And you know, we didn't know what was going on. But then we started to see some, you know, small, you know, dots running. And the sound was getting bigger in volume. And this Sergeant Kunz-- we were about 150 or so people in this group-- and he ordered us immediately to lay down on the ground face down. We had about 30 guards with us, approximately, with sub-machine guns or, or regular guns and dogs. And he left two guards guarding us. And he told them that if anybody lifts their head, to shoot them immediately. And he took the rest of the guards with him. There was-- not far from us, there was a pasture. And there were horses in this pasture. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a fence around it, sort of a wire fence, and bushes all around. And he took all these guards. And he put them in a blocking position. Because we could see in which direction these prisoners were running. And he put them right in front of this pasture. And I could see-- I was on the edge of this group. And I could sort of see what-- I was looking on the side, and I could see the prisoners. These guys were running, climbing over the fence and running into this pasture where the horses were. And they were running through it, in one big group, you know. And as they were coming to the end, I could hear the order, fire. And you know, and they all mowed down, you know. They didn't know. It was just-- it was their terrible misfortune that we happened to be working there that day. Because many more could have probably escaped. Because the forest were just not far from there. And I know from books that I read that only four escaped. And this was the Sonderkommando. This was a special group that was tending the crematorium in Birkenau. They blew up the crematorium when they were going to be killed themselves. Because usually the Sonderkommando was destroyed every 90 days. And they knew that their time was up. And they had some-- some kind of Molotov cocktails. And they had some explosive devices that they built. And when the SS unit came in to the crematorium, they started a fight, you know. They threw Molotov cocktails at them. And they broke through the gates, and they were running away. Unfortunately, only four escaped. So we were right away assembled. And we were marched back to Auschwitz. I remember going by this fence. There were people hanging from the fence, you know, with their-- terrible, you know, they were all dead, you know, with their heads open, you know, and terrible wounds. And we were marched back to Auschwitz. And, at that time, we were standing on appell, on to be-- on counting, all night. Because they could not account for all the missing bodies, you see. And this was our punishment, you know, that we were sending there, practically all night on appell. And you know, you were just standing there like a rock, you know, tired, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: So a few days after that, there were selections that started in Auschwitz. And the selection meant that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Russians, not that far away, the people had no more work to do. The German factories were packing up their equipment and their machinery and shipping it back to Germany. And so there were-- there was a lot of excess people here, that they didn't want to feed anymore. So they-- the selections were-- this selection was done, this particular one was done in the middle of the night. And I think there were 40 barracks in Auschwitz. And they went from barrack to barrack, one barrack first.

speaker: interviewee
text: They woke you up in the middle of the night. They started at a certain time. And you had to run naked through the camp, to this-- to the [GERMAN], to the laundry place. I think it was a big shed. And these SS officers were standing there. And you had to run in front of them. They were looking at your body, and-- just like cattle, you know. And if they saw that your body was really far gone, they just took your number down, and you were taken immediately to a quarantine block-- a quarantine barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I told you, my father and uncle were in a different barrack than I was. So when my turn came, I was woken up from this-- you know, you were sleeping the sleep of the dead, you know, after a hard day's work. And all a sudden, you know, you're woken up. [GERMAN], [GERMAN], and they scream and yell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they drive you down. And you run, you know, in a single lane. And you go in front of these officers. And I remember there was a guy stopped in front of me. And his number was being taken. And I didn't stop, I just kept running behind him, you know. I just-- an instinctive thing, you know, that had told me to go. And I went, and I went through. I came out at the other end of the [GERMAN], you know, the laundry. And I went back to my barrack. And I didn't know what happened to my father and uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So next morning when we were-- after the gong was on-- because at night, you could not walk around in this camp, you know. It was like a curfew, you know. You had to be in your bunk. And the camp was patrolled by SS with dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, I had to wait until morning, when we were allowed to get out of bed. I went to my father's barrack. And they were not in their bunks, they were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I immediately ran to-- to the quarantine barrack. And there were other boys like myself. And we were yelling out for names, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father and my uncle, they noticed me, and I noticed them. And they came to the fence. And this was, sort of, I would think, middle of October of '44. And you know, we knew. They knew what was happening. And I knew, too. But we were still hoping. Because they usually told you that they're taking you to another camp. But you know, I knew that they were taking them to Birkenau, to the gas chambers. And they knew that too. And my father had a small, little prayer book that he managed to get a hold of from a girl in this [NON-ENGLISH], you know. And he gave me this prayer book. And he told me to never forget that you are a Jew, you know. And we said goodbye. And this was the last time I saw them. And I really felt lost there. Because they were a tremendous support group for me. And you know, I would have never survived, if I didn't have them all this time. You know, they helped me with food, part of their rations, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: So from here on, I was really on my own. And that was a frightening thought, you know, in a place like this. And I had to leave them. And I had to go to my work detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember for days, when I came back that night from the work detail, and they were gone already. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whenever I was working outside Birkenau, you know, we could always see these chimneys, these five crematoriums, you know, going full tilt. Especially in '44, when the Hungarian Jews were being gassed, you know, 500,000 within four months. I mean, the crematoriums were going around the clock. And I could always just picture-- I was looking at these flames and fires going out. And I sort of always pictured the, the faces of my parents, you know, there, you know. You could never escape from this. You always saw these flames and the smoke.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about a week later, I was on another working detail. And this time, we were, again, leveling ground. They cut some trees down, and they dynamited the roots of these trees.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two of us were detailed to every tree trunk, you know, the roots, to dig it out. There were two of us, another young boy like myself. We each had shovels. And we had to start to dig the roots. And, as I told you, we were always cordoned around, with SS guards. And you know, you were loafing around, when you didn't have-- you looked around. And when you didn't see any guards, you didn't work too hard. Because you know, hard work and no food, you know, it would-- you know, it kills you faster, you know. So when you didn't have to work, you didn't work, you know. They called it sabotage. They could just hit you over the head or shoot you on the spot for this. But you know, we sabotaged, you know. And that was the way we could sabotage, by not working that hard. So--
